title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Tata Consultancy Services Limited
keyArguments: The petitioner was terminated following a duly conducted domestic inquiry which adhered to principles of natural justice, including adequate notice and opportunity to be heard.
The petitioner was found guilty of misappropriation of confidential client data in violation of the company's Code of Conduct and IT Security Policy, constituting gross misconduct.
The respondent employer followed the standing orders applicable to the establishment and the termination was neither arbitrary nor mala fide.
The petitioner failed to produce any credible evidence to rebut the findings of the Inquiry Officer, whose report was based on documentary and electronic evidence.
The Labour Court's order setting aside the termination was perverse and contrary to settled principles of law governing judicial interference in domestic inquiries.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Bombay set aside the Labour Court's order reinstating the petitioner-employee and upheld the termination order passed by Tata Consultancy Services Limited. The Court held that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly, the findings of misconduct were supported by evidence, and the Labour Court had erred in substituting its own view for that of the Inquiry Officer without finding any procedural infirmity.
judgmentText:
JUDGMENT

Date of Pronouncement: 14th March, 2023

Coram: Hon'ble Justice Priya Nair

Writ Petition No. 4412 of 2022

This writ petition challenges the award dated 22nd August, 2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. III, Mumbai, whereby the termination of the petitioner, Ramesh Kumar Sharma, from the services of the respondent company, Tata Consultancy Services Limited, was set aside with a direction for reinstatement with 50% back wages.

FACTS IN BRIEF:

The petitioner was employed as a Senior Systems Analyst with the respondent since 2009. In January 2020, a complaint was received from a client alleging unauthorized access and extraction of confidential project data. Following an internal investigation, a charge-sheet was issued to the petitioner on 15th March, 2020, alleging violations of the company's IT Security Policy and Code of Conduct. A domestic inquiry was conducted by an independent Inquiry Officer. The petitioner was represented by a co-worker of his choice during the proceedings. The Inquiry Officer, upon examination of electronic access logs, email records, and oral testimony of witnesses, returned a finding of guilt. Accordingly, the respondent passed an order of dismissal on 10th July, 2020. The petitioner raised an industrial dispute, and the Labour Court, by its award, set aside the dismissal on the ground that the punishment was disproportionate.

CONTENTIONS:

Learned counsel for the petitioner-employer submitted that the Labour Court committed a grave error in interfering with the quantum of punishment without first returning a finding that the inquiry was vitiated or that the findings were perverse. Reliance was placed upon the Supreme Court's decision in B.C. Chaturvedi v. Union of India (1995) 6 SCC 749, wherein it was held that the scope of judicial review in disciplinary matters is limited and courts cannot substitute their own view on the adequacy of punishment unless it is shockingly disproportionate.

Counsel for the respondent-employee argued that the punishment of dismissal was harsh for a first-time offence and that the Labour Court rightly exercised its discretion.

FINDINGS:

This Court has carefully perused the inquiry record and the impugned award. The domestic inquiry was conducted in accordance with the principles of natural justice. The petitioner was given a full and fair opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and lead his own defence. The findings of the Inquiry Officer are based on electronic evidence in the form of access logs which are contemporaneous records and carry significant evidentiary value. The act of extracting confidential client data is not a minor infraction. It strikes at the root of the trust reposed by clients in the employer and has direct commercial and legal consequences.

The Labour Court proceeded on the erroneous premise that it could reassess the proportionality of punishment without first recording a finding of procedural irregularity or perversity in the inquiry findings. This approach is contrary to the law laid down by the Supreme Court in Regional Manager, U.P. SRTC v. Hoti Lal (2003) 4 SCC 370.

In view of the foregoing, this Court finds that the impugned award of the Labour Court is unsustainable in law.

ORDER:

The writ petition is allowed. The award dated 22nd August, 2022 passed by the Labour Court No. III, Mumbai is hereby quashed and set aside. The order of dismissal dated 10th July, 2020 passed by the respondent-employer is restored. There shall be no order as to costs.

(Justice Priya Nair)
High Court of Bombay
